main_summary.summary: Ben Caldwell, born 1945 in Deming, New Mexico, recounts his trajectory from a multiracial border town shaped by Guadalupe Hidalgo, Pancho Villa, Geronimo, and Pershing-era history through Vietnam, UCLA's L.A. Rebellion film cohort, and four decades of cultural organizing in Leimert Park's Crenshaw district. He describes his projectionist grandfather who navigated Latino, white, and Black-balcony theaters; segregated schooling that forced him into a one-block-misplaced 'all-white' classroom; and the family ethic of pride and self-defense that carried him into the Army and Vietnam, where reading Malcolm X stripped away the religious veil and taught him to be 'a pacifist in a war zone.' At Phoenix College and Arizona State he abandoned football and economics, eventually entering UCLA's MFA program where he joined Haile Gerima and roughly thirteen other students of color in what became the L.A. Rebellion. Caldwell traces the founding of KAOS Network (originally Video 3333) in Leimert Park, his teaching at Howard University with Bernice Johnson Reagon and Alan Lomax, his films Medea, Babylon Is Falling, I & I, and I-Phresh, his Cuban Babalawo and yoga research with Yogi Bhajan, the 1992 Rodney King uprising he witnessed, and ongoing Sankofa-principles hip-hop pedagogy with Project Blowed.
key_themes: KAOS Network as community media laboratory
1992 Los Angeles uprising and CIA-crack allegations
Cross-cultural confluence in art versus politics
main_summary.historical_significance:
Caldwell is one of the founding figures of the L.A. Rebellion, the UCLA-anchored Black independent film movement that produced Haile Gerima, Charles Burnett, Julie Dash, Jamaa Fanaka, and others. His testimony connects three distinct geographies of Black American history that rarely sit in one oral history: the Black-Hispanic Southwest (Calle de los Negros, Calafia, Black Moors, Mimbres), the Vietnam-era draftee experience of seeing oneself as 'the other' chasing 'the other,' and the post-1970s Crenshaw cultural economy where he founded KAOS Network and Project Blowed.

The interview also documents Caldwell's first-person account of the 1992 Rodney King uprising from his Leimert Park location, his collaborations with Worth W. Long, John Outterbridge's Communicative Arts Academy, Shirley Clarke's democratic-media pedagogy, Bernice Johnson Reagon and Alan Lomax (via Bill Barlow's Howard University circle), and the Norton Family Foundation's Phantom Galleries program. His framing of hip-hop as a Sankofa-principles archival remix, and of capoeira / quilombos / Rastafarian resistance as the African diasporic continuum, gives the LoC Civil Rights History Project corpus its strongest cultural-worker / artist-as-organizer testimony for the post-Movement West Coast.

chapters.title: Howard, Bernice Reagon, Lomax
chapters.summary: Caldwell opens by recalling his three years teaching at Howard University, working at the Smithsonian with Bernice Johnson Reagon, meeting Alan Lomax through Dr. William Barlow ('Bill Barlow, the doctor of the blues'), and encountering Worth W. Long at the Robert Flaherty Film Seminar in Philadelphia where Lomax's The Land Where the Blues Began deeply moved him.
keywords: The Land Where the Blues Began

chapters.title: Migrant Family, Deming Border History
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes his Black-Hispanic migrant farming family across Texas, Oklahoma, New Mexico, and Arizona, and his grandfather who could 'pass as Latino,' acquired property, met the mayor, and projected films in segregated theaters (Latino, white, Black-balcony). He locates Deming, New Mexico at the Guadalupe Hidalgo border zone with Pancho Villa, Geronimo, Cochise, the Butterfield Overland Mail, and Pershing's first wartime use of airplanes.

chapters.title: Childhood, Schooling, Pride
chapters.summary: Caldwell recounts colored-team semi-pro baseball circuits across the Southwest, his foreman father born in Des Moines who attended Marquette, storytellers performing the Signifying Monkey, and being placed by one-block geography in the all-white Smith school, where rock-throwing peers taught him to fight back. He frames the family ethic his father transmitted: stand up for yourself, do not cow-tail, and require equal address.

chapters.title: Vietnam, Pacifism, Malcolm X
chapters.summary: Drafted out of Arizona School of Art at 21, Caldwell describes refusing exploratory knee surgery, going to Japan, dining with the U.S. ambassador and the theater general (Westmoreland's predecessor), then returning to Vietnam for the Tet Offensive. He recounts shooting wide of an old man to spare his village from napalm, reading Malcolm X, recognizing Vietnamese people and the Montagnards as 'the other' just as he was the other in the United States, and learning to be 'a pacifist in a war zone.'

chapters.title: Phoenix College, Photography, UCLA
chapters.summary: Returning home, Caldwell briefly played football at Phoenix College and Arizona State before quitting over racial nicknaming and his concern about long-term knee damage. He studied economics until its predictive failures regarding the Depression alienated him, then committed to photographing his family and the Arizona landscape, observing the tonal continuity between Black skin and desert earth. That series won him a full scholarship to UCLA's MFA program, where he encountered a defected KGB instructor in a revolutionary-philosophy course.

chapters.title: Watts, Outterbridge, L.A. Arts Fusion
chapters.summary: Coming to Los Angeles in 1971, Caldwell worked at the Watts Towers under John Outterbridge at the Communicative Arts Academy, the first media-arts-fusion organization in the area. He describes the holistic L.A. arts movement of theater, film, painting, music, and poetry, places like The Jua collective, drum classes with Nigerian masters, food co-ops, and the concentration of Motown-era performers (Miracles, Temptations, Richard Pryor) in the Crenshaw neighborhood. He encountered Calle de los Negros and the Black-Hispanic colonization of California, co-teaching with Norman Klein on 'California, the history of forgetting.'

chapters.title: L.A. Rebellion, Haile Gerima
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes UCLA's segregation of ethnographic-film and regular-film tracks, his cohort being the first students of color admitted to the regular program, and how they gravitated around Haile Gerima as TA. He frames the L.A. Rebellion (initially about 13, growing to 22 over a decade and producing 55 films) as a rebellion against blaxploitation, distinguishing the original Black-authored Sweetback, Shaft, and Superfly from the white-imitator exploitation cycle that followed. He cites his films Medea (1971), I & I, Babylon Is Falling: A Visual Ritual for Peace (1980), and The Nubian.

chapters.title: KAOS Network, Hip-Hop, I-Phresh
chapters.summary: Caldwell founded Video 3333 (later KAOS Network) in Leimert Park to operate as an artist-advocate practice for 'the poor and the vulnerable' outside Hollywood. Working with Charles Burnett, he targeted 11-25-year-olds as the cultural-generative cohort, documented the emerging hip-hop lexicon, and made the 1989 short I-Phresh fusing youth-movement and Rastafarian framings. He describes hip-hop as Sankofa-principles remix and archival research, James Brown drum-break sampling as homage, capoeira as the encoded martial art behind break dancing, and quilombos / Rastafarian hill-resistance as African diasporic continuity.

chapters.title: Cuba, Babalawos, Yogi Bhajan
chapters.summary: In 1999 Caldwell joined a Yogi Bhajan-sponsored delegation of Black yoga teachers to Cuba to study how Yoruba culture and Babalawo priesthood preserved African religious science across the Middle Passage. He describes Cuban physicists studying tribal markings as acupuncture immunization points, the Cuban state's deployment of healers and 300,000 soldiers against apartheid in Angola / South Africa, and bureaucratic encounters with state-owned nightclubs and DJs. He frames hip-hop exchange with Cuban hip-hop heads, and notes Los Muñequitos de Matanzas's New Orleans connection.

chapters.title: 1992 Uprising, Rodney King
chapters.summary: Caldwell recounts witnessing the 1992 Los Angeles uprising from Leimert Park: the shock of the Rodney King verdict, a white kid apologizing on the corner before driving south, multi-ethnic anarchy including Central American señoritas hauling crates from looted stores, neighbors physically rescuing a Black gallery's African sculptures, and the Korean grocer-family being protected by Black neighbors after the broader Korean-Black tension. He invokes the Charles R. Drew Postgraduate Medical School / Martin Luther King doctors' research linking gang infrastructure to organized covert distribution, citing the CIA / Dark Alliance crack allegations as connecting context.

chapters.title: Sankofa, Payphones, Phantom Galleries
chapters.summary: Caldwell describes his current Sankofa-principles project with USC School of Innovation graduate students to repurpose payphones as Wi-Fi totems and hyper-local cultural-distribution nodes, partnering with LA Metro and Pacific Bell on virtual-space anti-gentrification design. He details the Phantom Galleries program (Norton Family Foundation anchor) that filled nine derelict Crenshaw buildings with art, the Electronic Café International project he ran at the 1984 Los Angeles Olympics connecting five disparate communities via proto-internet teleconferencing, and the mapping of cultural density showing every major Black musical figure lived or worked within a five-mile radius (Miles Davis, Billie Holiday, Ella Fitzgerald, Hampton Hawes, The Chambers Brothers).

chapters.title: Art Versus Politics, Closing
chapters.summary: Caldwell closes by articulating his lifelong refusal to align with political camps in favor of an artist's cross-cultural communicability, citing his teaching of Nazis at CalArts, his admiration for Leni Riefenstahl's craft despite her ideology, and his rejection of communism after reading The Communist Manifesto's framing of the lumpenproletariat ('that's my mama'). He frames his work as 'making flowers out of compost' and credits Egyptian-encoded musical cosmology as the holographic carrier of African historical memory through enslavement.

loc_video.caption: Ben Caldwell oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Los Angeles, California, 2013 April 11
